coredata.dc:title: Synergetic assembly of a molybdenum disulfide/carbon quantum dot heterojunction with enhanced light absorption and electron transfer di-functional properties for photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Yanning
Li, Xinyang
Cui, Mei
Huang, Renliang
Ma, Wanquan
Wang, Yunting
Su, Rongxin
Qi, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Cocatalysts play important roles in enhanced efficiency of heterojunctions that their selection and synthesis are essential. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one of important cocatalysts, often suffer from complicated synthesis, low functionality, and low photocatalytic efficiency issues that need to be solved effectively. In this study, we constructed a new molybdenum disulfide/N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (MoS2/N,S-CQDs) heterojunction with enhanced light absorption and electrons transfer di-functional properties via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The MoS2/N,S-CQDs simultaneously degraded 99.8% of methylene blue (MB) and 95.1% of malachite green (MG) in an actual water system (Tianjin Lake) under simulated sunlight irradiation for 120 min. The heterojunction showed obvious efficiencies under both infrared light (degraded 83.8% of MG after 120 min) and visible light (90.1% MG after 120 min) irradiation. According to energy level and density functional theory (DFT) analyses, the electrons in MoS2/N,S-CQDs were demonstrated that transferred from N,S-CQDs to MoS2 interfaces, and the light-absorption range of the composites was effectively extended to near-infrared ranges owing to excellent up-conversion fluorescence property of the N,S-CQDs. We believe that the facile synthetic technique and effective multifunctional properties of composite have the potential for further research on CQDs-based heterojunctions and their industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Molybdenum disulfide
Photocatalytic degradation
Di-functional
Electrons transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs)
Degradation of pollutants in actual water systems
Photocatalytic reusability and reactive species analyses

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976132 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22308205 ) and the Key Scientific Research Project in Shanxi Province (No. 202201090301014 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a-b) MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.3, (c-d) MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5, and (e-f) MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.7 materials.
(a-b) TEM and HRTEM images and (c-f) elemental mapping images of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 composite.
XRD patterns of MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDsx materials.
(a) Full XPS spectrum and high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) Mo 3d, and (d) S 2p in MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5.
Photocatalytic activity and pseudo-first-order kinetics of MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDsx for degradation of (a-b) MB and (c-d) MG under visible-light irradiation.

(a-b) Photocatalytic reusability tests, (c-d) the capture experiments of active species during degradation of MB with MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5, ESR signals of (e) DMPO-∙O- 2 and (f) DMPO-∙OH adducts with above materials under simulated solar irradiation.

(a) UV–Vis–NIR diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) corresponding plots of (Ahν) 2 versus (hv), (c) transient current response, and (d) electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDsx materials.

The planar-averaged electron density differences along Z direction after DFT calculation (in which the blue regions indicates electron consumption and the yellow regions represents electron accumulation). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic synthesis illustrating of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs composite.
Schematic of photocatalytic mechanisms in MoS2/N,S-CQDs heterojunction.

head.ce:title: Synergetic assembly of a molybdenum disulfide/carbon quantum dot heterojunction with enhanced light absorption and electron transfer di-functional properties for photocatalysis

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cocatalysts play important roles in enhanced efficiency of heterojunctions that their selection and synthesis are essential. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one of important cocatalysts, often suffer from complicated synthesis, low functionality, and low photocatalytic efficiency issues that need to be solved effectively. In this study, we constructed a new molybdenum disulfide/N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (MoS2/N,S-CQDs) heterojunction with enhanced light absorption and electrons transfer di-functional properties via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The MoS2/N,S-CQDs simultaneously degraded 99.8% of methylene blue (MB) and 95.1% of malachite green (MG) in an actual water system (Tianjin Lake) under simulated sunlight irradiation for 120 min. The heterojunction showed obvious efficiencies under both infrared light (degraded 83.8% of MG after 120 min) and visible light (90.1% MG after 120 min) irradiation. According to energy level and density functional theory (DFT) analyses, the electrons in MoS2/N,S-CQDs were demonstrated that transferred from N,S-CQDs to MoS2 interfaces, and the light-absorption range of the composites was effectively extended to near-infrared ranges owing to excellent up-conversion fluorescence property of the N,S-CQDs. We believe that the facile synthetic technique and effective multifunctional properties of composite have the potential for further research on CQDs-based heterojunctions and their industrial applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry and agriculture, a large number of harmful organic compounds have been released into the environment, increasing the risk of water pollution and eliciting international concern [1–3]. The majority of organic pollutants are dispersed in rivers, lakes, oceans, and even drinking water, resulting in adverse health effects (e.g., carcinogenicity and mutagenicity). Dyes are highly emitted organic pollutants that are difficult to handle in industry. Approximately 280,000 tons of textile dyes are discharged into sewage in China every year, posing a significant threat to the aquatic environment [4–6]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop efficient, environmentally friendly technologies for degrading dyes in wastewater, in which the photocatalytic oxidation is one of promising methods.

To date, a variety of photocatalysts have been employed for pollutant degradation. In addition to traditional oxide photocatalysts (such as TiO2 [7], ZnO [8], Cu2O [9], Fe2O3 [10], etc.), sulphides (such as MoS2 [11], CdS [12], ZnIn2S4 [13], etc.) have received increasing attention. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a typical two-dimensional (2D) layered transition-metal sulphide, is widely used in photocatalysis [14]. According to previous reports, bulk MoS2 is an indirect-bandgap semiconductor (1.29 eV) that can form a direct-bandgap semiconductor (1.90 eV) in monolayer structures [15,16]. In general, although the narrow bandgaps of MoS2 benefit from its light absorption, its unmatched energy levels make photocatalytic oxidation and reduction difficult. The addition of cocatalysts is an important strategy for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of heterojunction materials. Notably, choosing appropriate cocatalysts and simple yet effective assembly techniques for composites are crucial for facilitating their industrial applications and must be researched further.

Carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in sensors, bioimaging, and photocatalysis [17,18]. CQDs are low in cost and toxicity, possess up-conversion fluorescence and photogenerated electron-transfer properties; the latter two properties play important roles in enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [19–21]. Compared to traditional photocatalysts (such as ZnO, TiO2 etc., as shown in Table S1), combining CQDs with MoS2 can better solve the narrow light absorption and poor electron-transfer ability of photocatalysts. Zhao et al. constructed CQDs/MoS2 composites using a hydrothermal method and applied them as catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [22]. Although the composites showed enhanced efficiency compared to single MoS2, their assembly as well as the photocatalytic mechanisms occurring between the CQDs and MoS2 were unclear. Li et al. synthesised a CQDs@2D MoS2 heterostructure via a sonication–hydrothermal method [23], in which electrons were transferred from MoS2 to CDs; however, the light-absorption ability was not effectively improved, particularly for near-infrared (NIR) light. Chen et al. constructed a CQDs/g-C3N4/MoS2 ternary heterojunction for HER and rhodamine B (RhB) degradation [24], in which the degradation efficiency of ternary material was better than that of pristine g-C3N4; however, the synthetic method was too complicated to be applied in industry. According to previous reports, except for the simplified synthesis, the multifunctional properties (including the light absorption and separation abilities of electrons and holes) of composites with cocatalysts (such as CQDs) are crucial for construction of MoS2-based materials, and it is essential to build high-efficiency CQDs and strengthen their advantages during photocatalytic procedures.

In this study, a new type of surface-functionalized N,S-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) with enhanced light absorption and electron-transfer functional properties was synthesised and combined with MoS2 to construct MoS2/N, S-CQD heterojunctions via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. Their surface morphologies and structural properties were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, and their photocatalytic performances were investigated via photodegradation of model pollutants, methylene blue (MB) and malachite green (MG), under visible light (Vis), near-infrared light (NIR), and simulated sunlight irradiation, respectively. The light absorption and electron-transfer properties of the composites were investigated and compared with that of single MoS2, and their photocatalytic mechanisms were summarised after active species detection and efficiency analyses through experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations in detail.

Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4) was obtained from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). Thiourea (TU, CSN2H4), ethylene glycol (EG), methylene blue (MB) and malachite green (MG) were purchased from Guangfu Chemical Co. (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO), and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Yuanli Chemical Co. (Tianjin, China). Absolute ethanol (C2H5OH) was purchased from Concord Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). L -Cysteine (C3H7NO2S) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All these chemicals were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1.5 g of L-cysteine was added to 20 mL of the reaction system (9.00 mL of ethylene glycol, 4.75 mL of deionized water, and 6.25 mL of concentrated (68 wt%) nitric acid), and the mixture was stirred vigorously to dissolve the components. The resulting solution was transferred to a reaction vessel and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling to room temperature (25 °C), the mixture was centrifugated, and the supernatant was dialysed (MWCO 3500) for 2–3 days and stored at 4 °C.

A previously described one-pot hydrothermal method was used [20]. Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4) and thiourea (0.342 g, CSN2H4) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionised water and magnetically stirred for 10 min until complete dissolution. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 5.0 with HCl, and the dialysed N,S-CQDs were added to the system at different mass ratios, with vigorous stirring for 10 min. The precursor solution was transferred to a reaction vessel and reacted at 200 °C for 24 h. To remove impurities, the product was washed with deionised water and absolute ethanol for three times. The washed samples were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h. The mass ratios of the N,S-CQDs to MoS2 (0.3, 0.5, and 0.7) are denoted as MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.3, MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5, and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.7, respectively.

The photocatalytic performances of above-mentioned materials were evaluated by degrading a series of simulated organic pollutants under visible, NIR, and simulated solar light irradiations, respectively. Specifically, 5 mg of MoS2/N,S-CQDs was placed in a photocatalytic device containing 50 mL of a 20 mg/L organic pollutant solution. A 20 cm distance was maintained between the reaction system and Xe lamp (300 W). Samples were collected at specific time intervals, during which the solid catalyst was removed by centrifugation, and the corresponding absorbance of the supernatant was measured with a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) equipped with an ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of pollutants in actual water systems
ce:section.ce:para: Three types of actual water samples were collected from Tianjin University Lake, Tianjin Lake, and underground water (Tianjin City). The water samples were pretreated by bubbling with an inert gas (N2) to remove harmful gases, and then filtered through a microporous filter membrane (50 mm, 0.45 μm) [25]. Subsequently, the MB and MG contaminants were dissolved in different water samples to form one- or two-ingredient simulated contaminant systems. The photocatalyst (5 mg) was added to the organic pollutant solutions (50 mL, 20 mg/L) and reacted under simulated sunlight irradiation, as described in Section 2.4.

All the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab Initio Package (VASP) software within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of PBE formulation. Projected augmented wave (PAW) potentials were chosen to describe the ionic cores using valence electrons on a plane-wave basis, with a kinetic energy cutoff of 520 eV. The partial occupancies of the Kohn–Sham 3×3×1 orbitals were determined using a Gaussian smearing method with a width of 0.05 eV. The electronic energy was considered as a self-consistent state when it was lower than 10−5 eV, and the geometry optimisation was convergent when the force change was lower than 0.02 eV/Å.

The morphologies of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were observed using field-emission SEM (S4800, Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Japan) and TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL Co., Japan). The XRD patterns were determined using an X-ray diffractometer (D8-Focus, Bruker Co., Germany) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source at 2θ = 10–70° and a scan rate of 6°/min. Elemental analysis of the composite was carried out using XPS (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., U.K.). The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of the nanoflowers were calculated using a fully automated specific surface and porosity analyser (BET, ASAP 2460, Micromeritics, USA) after N2 adsorption–desorption testing. The light-absorption properties of the materials were detected using a UV–Vis/NIR diffuse reflectance spectrometer (UV-3600i Plus, Shimadzu, Japan) with BaSO4 as the benchmark, and the test wavelength range was 200–2500 nm. Transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance measurements were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Gamry Co., America), in which a counter electrode (platinum electrode), reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), working electrode (ITO glass with samples), and electrolyte (2.5 mmol/L potassium ferricyanide solution) were employed. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of above samples were detected through a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS5). The electron paramagnetic resonance spectra (ESR) of the composites were determined using an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EMX Plus, Bruker Co., Germany), in which the spin trap of the superoxide radical (∙O- 2) and hydroxyl radical (∙OH) was 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO).

The synthetic mechanism of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composite is shown in Scheme 1 . N,S-CQDs were added to the precursors with molybdenum and sulphur sources directly for the construction of composite using a one-pot hydrothermal method. After crystal growth and assembly, the MoS2 nanosheets were gradually formed and stabilised through nanoflower-like structures loaded with N,S-CQDs on their surfaces. According to our previous reports, L-cysteine and polyethylene glycol were employed as carbon sources and surface modifiers for the construction of N,S-CQDs with –NH2 and –OH on their surfaces, which was beneficial for the assembly of MoS2 and N,S-CQDs via physical absorption and hydrogen-bonding connections [20,26].

The surface structures of the as-prepared MoS2/N,S-CQDs materials were observed using SEM images. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 composite possessed a uniformly distributed nanoflower-like spherical structure with an average diameter of ∼550 nm, which was similar to the configuration of single MoS2 (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). With an increasing mass ratio of N,S-CQDs to MoS2, the spherical structures gradually became dispersed, as shown in Fig. 1c and 1e. These results may be attributed to increased surface energy between MoS2 nanosheets under the influence of greater amount of N,S-CQDs, which restricted the structural variations of MoS2 from nanosheets to nanoflowers (with the lower surface energy) [27,28]. Although the nanoflower-like structures of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.7 become looser, their structural dispersity and exposed active sites were affected to a certain degree.

The microscopic structures of composites were further analysed using TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 , the MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 composite showed distinct lattice stripes of 0.32 nm and 0.27 nm, which corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of N,S-CQDs and (100) crystal plane of MoS2, respectively. Moreover, the elemental distribution of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5, as shown in Fig. 2c-f, reveals that C, Mo, and S were uniformly distributed in the selected regions. These results demonstrate that the N,S-CQDs were effectively loaded onto surfaces of MoS2 material [29].

The crystal structures of MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs with different mass ratios were characterised using XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 3 , the diffraction peaks of these samples were similar and located at 14.1°, 32.9°, 33.7°, 36.0°, 39.5°, 43.3°, and 58.8°, respectively, which corresponded to the (002), (100), (101), (102), (103), (006), and (110) planes of a standard hexagonal MoS2 structure (PDF#75-1539), indicating the formation of MoS2 in the composites [22]. The intensities of diffraction peaks of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 were slightly stronger than those of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.3, MoS2/N, and S-CQDs0.7, indicating better crystallisation of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 material. Owing to the relatively low amount of N,S-CQDs, there were no evident diffraction peaks of the cocatalyst, which is consistent with previous reports [21].

The elemental compositions of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were investigated using XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 a, MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 contained mainly C, O, Mo, S, and N, indicating a specific composition of MoS2 and N,S-CQDs. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b), the typical peaks located at 284.1, 285.8, and 288.1 eV correspond to CC, COC, and OCO bonds, respectively, which exist in ethylene glycol units and carbonyl structures on N,S-CQDs [26,30]. The high-resolution Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 4c) shows two peaks at 227.9 and 231.3 eV, corresponding to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 orbitals, respectively [22,31]. In the high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4d), two distinct peaks appear near 160.9 eV and 162.1 eV, which correspond to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively, originating from the MoS bond in MoS2. These results demonstrate the effective assembly of MoS2 with N,S-CQDs [32].

To characterise the specific surface areas and pore-size distributions of the as-prepared photocatalysts, N2 adsorption–desorption tests were carried out, as shown in Fig. S2. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 showed type IV curves with adsorption hysteresis loops, which was assigned to mesoporous materials [33]. The specific surface areas of MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 were calculated to be 6.987 and 8.077 m2/g, respectively, and their average pore sizes were 9.461 and 10.266 nm, respectively. The mesoporous configurations were probably formed via cross-stacking MoS2 nanosheets on the composites, in which MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 presented a higher specific surface area than that of MoS2, benefitting the absorption of reactants during the photocatalytic reaction.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activities of as-prepared MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites, the photodegradation efficiencies of model pollutants (MB and MG) were employed under different light-irradiation conditions. The pollutant degradation rates (D%) were calculated using the following equation:
(1)
D
( % )
=
(
A
0
−
A
t
)
/
A
0
×
100 % =
(
C
0
−
C
t
)
/
C
0
×
100 %
where A0 and At represent the initial absorbance of the pollutants and the absorbance after a certain time (t), respectively; C0 and Ct represent the initial concentration of pollutants and the concentration after a certain time (t), respectively. Considering the effects of catalysts on the adsorption of pollutants without light irradiation, we conducted experiments on the adsorption of MB and MG by MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites under dark conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, the pollutant molecules reached adsorption–desorption equilibrium at around 60 min, in which ∼65.2% of MB and 64.7% of MG were adsorbed by the MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 after 60 min, respectively. This result might be closely related to the larger specific surface area and stable structure derived from specific physical absorption and partial chemical interactions between MoS2 and N,S-CQDs [29,34].

Photodegradation experiments under visible-light irradiation were carried out, as shown in Fig. 5 . The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of MB and MG by the MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were investigated, and it was found that the variation in ln(Ct/C0) with reaction time followed first-order kinetics:
(2)
ln
(
C
t
/
C
0
)
= − kt
where C0 and Ct represent the initial concentration of pollutants and the concentration after a certain time, respectively; k is the kinetic constant; and t is the reaction time. It was found that MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 degraded 96.9% of MB and 93.7% of MG, respectively, after 150 min. The first-order kinetic constants for the photodegradation of MB and MG by MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 are 0.0169 min–1 and 0.0123 min–1, which are ∼1.6 and 3.2-fold that of MoS2, respectively (Table S2). Moreover, with increasing N,S-CQDs amount, the photocatalytic activities of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites noticeably improved. When the mass ratio of N,S-CQDs to MoS2 was increased to 0.7, the photocatalytic activity decreased, which might be attributed to a certain amount of aggregation and active-site restriction with excessive N,S-CQDs [35,36].

The effects of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites on photodegradation of MB and MG under NIR light were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S4a, 74.2% of MB was degraded by MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 after 180 min irradiation of NIR light, and 83.8% of MG was degraded by the composite after NIR light irradiation of 120 min, which were all higher than those of single MoS2. From Fig. S4c, the effects of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites on photodegradation of MB and MG under simulated sunlight were investigated. The MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 degraded 98.9% of MB after 120 min and 91.1% of MG after 90 min. The corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic constants of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 for MB and MG photodegradation were 0.0304 min–1 and 0.0177 min–1, respectively, which were ∼1.9 and 1.6 times higher than that of MoS2 under simulated sunlight irradiation.

The kinetic constants (k) and fitting coefficients (R2) for the photodegradation of MB and MG by MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 under varying light irradiations are summarized in Table S2. It can be seen that the kinetic constant k of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 is higher than that of single MoS2 overall, indicating the significant effects of N,S-CQDs in MoS2/N,S-CQDs. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activities under simulated sunlight irradiation with MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 materials were higher than those under visible and NIR light, in which the up-conversion fluorescence of N,S-CQDs and the combination of the two components played important roles in their enhanced photocatalytic efficiencies under NIR light regions [37].

To investigate the actual application potential of the photocatalysts, we collected three types of environmental water samples for the photodegradation of different pollutants. The absorption and degradation periods were conducted simultaneously to simulate an industrial system with dyes. As shown in Fig. S5a, the degradation rates of MB by MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 after 60 min of simulated sunlight irradiation were 99.8%, 97.2%, and 90.5% in underground water, Tianjin University Lake (TJU Lake), and Tianjin Lake, respectively. In addition, a dual-pollutant system containing both MB and MG was prepared for Tianjin Lake, and the removal rates of MB and MG were 99.8% and 95.1%, respectively, after 120 min (Fig. S5b). These results indicate that the MoS2/N,S-CQDs have high photocatalytic efficiencies in actual water systems owing to their excellent absorption and degradation abilities, which provide many possibilities for industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reusability and reactive species analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Photodegradation recycling experiments were performed to investigate the stability of MoS2/N,S-CQD photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 6 a-b, the degraded MB with MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 still remained at 88.9% after five cycles of simulated sunlight irradiation, and its nanoflower-like structure was mostly retained (Fig. S6a). The XRD pattern after five cycles showed no significant changes in the characteristic peaks and their intensities (Fig. S6b), indicating that the composite exhibits good stability and reusability during the reaction.

Radical capture experiments were performed to investigate the active species during photocatalytic process of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 composite. As shown in Fig. 6c-d, the photodegradation efficiency of MB decreased significantly after the addition of isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and 1,4-p-benzoquinone (BQ), which captured the active species ∙OH, h+, and ∙O2 – during the reaction, respectively. The results indicated that the three kinds of active species significantly influenced the photodegradation efficiency of MB; the degradation activity of MB decreased from 98.9% (no scavenger) to 82.6% (IPA), 71.8% (AO), and 58.3% (BQ), in which ∙O2 – played a more important role than the other species during photodegradation.

The main active species produced by MoS2 and the MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were detected using ESR tests. As shown in Fig. 6e-f, both MoS2 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 exhibited certain ESR signals under the Xenon lamp irradiation, where the superoxide radical (∙O2 –) and hydroxyl radical (∙OH) signals of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 composite were significantly stronger than those of MoS2. These results indicate that these active species play important roles in photocatalytic reactions.

The light absorption and photoinduced carrier separation abilities of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were evaluated by conducting UV–Vis NIR diffuse reflectance and photoelectrical tests. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the light-absorption intensities of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were significantly stronger than those of MoS2, where MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 showed the strongest light absorption in the UV, Vis, and NIR regions, indicating its broad-spectrum and effective light-absorption abilities. From Fig. 7b, energy gap values (Eg) were extrapolated from the diffuse reflection data. The Eg of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 significantly decreased from 1.86 eV (for MoS2) to 1.40 eV, and the Eg of N,S-CQDs was calculated as 1.24 eV using the corresponding diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. S7).

To investigate the separation ability of photo-induced electrons and holes in the heterojunction, photoelectrical tests were conducted via transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance tests (Fig. 7c-d). The photocurrent was stable and recycled several times with on–off light irradiation, in which MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 showed a higher photocurrent response than MoS2. Additionally, the arc radius in the EIS spectrum of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 was obviously smaller than that of MoS2, indicating that MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 possessed a higher photoexcited carrier separation rate than pure MoS2. The N,S-CQDs were considered as a good “electron warehouse” for electron storage and transfer during the reaction, effectively enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency [38,39].

Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of MoS2 and N,S-CQDs materials were obtained to detect recombination rates in photo-generated electron-hole pairs. It is known that the higher intensity of PL intensity is, the higher recombination efficiency of photo-induced carriers is in composite [21,33]. Due to lower PL intensity of MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 was, the material showed better separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers than that of pure MoS2 after addition of N,S-CQDs (as shown in Fig. S8). Additionally, the planar-averaged charge-density differences in MoS2/N,S-CQDs were investigated by performing density functional theory (DFT) calculations (as shown in Fig. 8 ), where the blue areas indicate electron consumption, and the yellow regions represent electron accumulation. According to the electron-distribution statistics, electrons are prone to transfer from the N,S-CQDs to MoS2, particularly at interfaces between MoS2 and N,S-CQDs [40,41]. These results clearly demonstrate the enhanced electron transfer after the introduction of N,S-CQDs cocatalyst. Aside from their enhanced light-absorption abilities, the N,S-CQDs played effective electron transfer roles in the MoS2/N,S-CQDs heterojunction.

As shown in Fig. S9, the specific energy band positions of MoS2 and N,S-CQDs were calculated using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), in which low (Elow) and high (Ehigh) kinetic energy cutoffs were obtained from the corresponding tangent lines. According to previous reports [42,43], the band positions (vs. vacuum level) can be calculated using the equations of EVBM = hν – (Ehigh – Elow) and ECBM = EVBM – Eg (where hν represents incident photon energy of 21.2 eV), and the absolute potential of the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is ∼4.44 V (25 °C). As a result, the valence band (VB) potentials (EVBM) of MoS2 and N,S-CQDs were calculated to be 3.77 and 0.58 V (vs. NHE), and their corresponding ECBM values (vs. NHE) were 1.91 and –0.66 V, respectively (Table S3).

Based on these results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for MoS2/N,S-CQDs composite is proposed. As shown in Scheme 2 , owing to their specific optical properties and energy band positions, the N,S-CQDs play two important roles in the heterojunction. On the one hand, N,S-CQDs possess effective up-conversion fluorescence property that can absorb NIR light (∼740–850 nm), and emit visible light with a wavelength of ∼380–640 nm that exactly fulfils the visible-light absorption range of MoS2 (with an Eg of 1.86 eV). On the other hand, according to the photoelectrical tests and DFT calculations, the N,S-CQDs can be considered an “electron warehouse” for electron storage and transfer, which are significant to photocatalytic applications within the system [44,45]. Therefore, the MoS2/N,S-CQDs is a kind of effective photocatalyst with multi-functional properties.

Because the different active species, including ∙O2 –, ∙OH and h+ all played certain roles in the system according to the experiments, the photocatalytic mechanism of MoS2/N,S-CQDs can be summarised as follows. Specifically, after light irradiation and switching of light-absorption ranges via N,S-CQDs, the composite ingredients produced photo-induced electrons and holes within the system. The h+ generated from VB of the materials can combine with H2O or OH– to form ∙OH, and the electrons combine with O2 to form ∙O2 – on MoS2 and N,S-CQDs surfaces. As a result, the active species ∙OH, h+, and ∙O2 – formed in the heterojunction and synergistically oxidised the pollutants [21,46]. The photocatalytic mechanism can be summarised as follows:

- CQDs +

products

Moreover, the photocatalytic efficiencies of other relevant photocatalysts for degradation of pollutants were summarized in comparison to those of MoS2/N,S-CQDs as shown in Table S4. Overall, the photocatalytic performance of MoS2/N,S-CQDs was superior or equal to that of MoS2-related or CQDs-based materials [29,47]. The use of N,S-CQDs endowed the composite with multifunctional properties, as derived from various characterisation techniques including DFT calculations, which showed evident potentials for industrial applications.

In this study, a new kind of molybdenum disulfide/carbon quantum dot (MoS2/N,S-CQDs) heterojunction with enhanced light absorption and electron-transfer di-functional abilities was constructed via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. According to the structural characterisations, the N,S-CQDs were successfully loaded onto surfaces of the MoS2 material. The photocatalytic performances of MoS2/N,S-CQDs composites were evaluated and compared via degradation experiments in both deionised and actual water. The first-order kinetic constants for the photodegradation of MB and MG using MoS2/N,S-CQDs0.5 were 0.0169 min–1 and 0.0123 min–1 under visible-light irradiation, respectively, which are ∼1.6 and 3.2-fold that of MoS2, respectively. According to the photoelectrical, energy level and DFT analyses, the electrons in composite were prone to transfer from N,S-CQDs to MoS2, particularly at interfaces between MoS2 and N,S-CQDs. Additionally, the light absorption of the composite was extended to NIR regions owing to introduction of N,S-CQDs with excellent up-conversion fluorescence properties. Overall, the N,S-CQDs played multi-functional properties in MoS2/N,S-CQDs on both light absorption and separation of photo-generated carriers abilities. Accordingly, we believe that the facile synthetic technique and effectively multi-functional properties of the composite were potential to more researches on CQDs-based heterojunctions and their applications in industry.

Yanning Qu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinyang Li: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mei Cui: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Renliang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Wanquan Ma: Investigation. Yunting Wang: Investigation. Rongxin Su: Writing – review & editing. Wei Qi: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
